conversation.message: I have been smoking cigarettes for 15 years, about a pack a day.
That's a significant amount of time and cigarettes - can you tell me, what's a typical day look like for you, in terms of when and where you usually smoke?
Mostly after meals and when I am stressed at work.

It sounds like those post-meal and work stress moments can be really tough times for you, making it hard to resist the urge to smoke. What is it about quitting smoking that's really motivating you right now, is it something specific that's happened or someone who's been encouraging you?
My doctor told me my lungs are getting worse. My kids also keep asking me to stop.

It sounds like your doctor's concern and your kids' requests are giving you a lot of motivation to quit. Have you ever tried taking a few deep breaths when you feel stressed or like you want to smoke, to see if it helps calm you down?
I tried nicotine patches once but I kept forgetting to put them on.

You're right, forgetting to put on the patches didn't seem to work for you. Have you thought about trying nicotine gum, which you can use discreetly and might be easier to incorporate into your daily routine?
I want to quit completely. I just do not know where to start.

You've been wanting to quit for a long time and it's great that you're feeling motivated now. Have you ever thought about what you did differently during those periods when you were able to cut back on smoking, even if it was just for a little while?
Maybe I can try keeping gum with me for after meals. That sounds doable.

It sounds like you're open to trying the nicotine gum, especially after meals, which is one of your known triggers. Would you be willing to try it for a day or two and see how it goes before we consider other strategies to help you stay on track?
I smoke bidis, about 20 a day for 30 years.

You've been smoking bidis for 30 years, that's quite a long time. What are some things that usually trigger you to smoke a bidi, is it when you're stressed, around others, or during certain activities?
I do not know if I really want to quit. My son keeps pushing me.

You feel unsure about quitting and are being strongly encouraged by your son. Can you tell me more about what's going on inside you when you hear your son talking about quitting - is it frustration, guilt, or something else?
I smoke most in the morning after tea and when I feel bored at home.

It sounds like having a cup of tea in the morning is a pretty strong trigger for you to smoke. Can you imagine delaying having that tea for just 10 minutes, and doing something else instead, like going for a short walk or drinking a glass of water?
I never tried anything before. I always thought I could stop on my own.

You've been smoking for so long that it's become a habitual part of your daily routine, and quitting might feel like giving up a familiar friend. Can you think of any times in the past when you managed to cut back on smoking or resisted the urge to smoke, even if it was just for a short while?
My breathing is getting bad lately. Maybe that is a reason to try.
You're starting to see the connection between your bad breathing and quitting smoking. Are you willing to try delaying your morning tea for a short while to see if that helps you manage the craving?
Okay I will think about it. Maybe small steps.

It sounds like you're open to taking small steps towards quitting, which is a great starting point. Would you be willing to write down some specific situations, like morning tea time or when you feel bored, where you think you might need a little extra help staying smoke-free?
I smoke about 5 cigarettes a day, been doing it for 2 years.
You've been smoking for 2 years, which is a significant milestone. What time of day do you usually have your first cigarette?
Mainly when I am with friends who smoke or during exam stress.

It sounds like social situations are a big trigger for you, and exam stress isn't helping either. Can you tell me what's holding you back from quitting now, even though you know it's not good for you?
I want to reduce first. I am not ready to quit completely yet.

You're still not ready to quit completely, but you want to reduce your smoking first - that's a good starting point. Would you be open to making a daily plan to help manage your cravings, especially in social situations and during exam stress?
I tried avoiding parties for a week but ended up smoking more during exams.

You've tried avoiding parties before, but that didn't seem to work, especially during exam stress. Are you open to trying something different, like having a non-smoking buddy with you in social situations to help keep you on track?
I think gum or something to keep my hands busy might help.
You've already got an idea about keeping your hands busy, which is a great start. Would you be willing to try drawing or doodling as a way to keep your hands occupied when you feel the urge to smoke?
I have been smoking two packs a day for 20 years.
Wow, two packs a day for 20 years is a significant habit. What's a typical day like for you - are there any particular times or situations when you find yourself craving a cigarette?
I smoke after every meal, with my morning coffee, and when I feel anxious.

It sounds like you have some strong routines that involve smoking, and it's clear how anxious you get when you feel like you need a cigarette. Can you tell me what worries you most about quitting smoking, and what's holding you back from doing it for good?
I want to quit because I had chest pain last month and my doctor is worried.

You're really motivated to quit for your health, and that's a great reason. Have you ever tried saying "no" to a cigarette offer from a friend or family member when you're feeling anxious, just to see if it's possible to decline and still feel okay?
I tried nicotine gum, patches, and cold turkey. None of them worked for long.

Since nicotine gum and patches didn't work for you in the past, do you think you'd be open to exploring some behavioral strategies, like replacing smoking with a new activity, to help break the habit?
I always go back when I get very stressed. That is my biggest problem.
You feel like stress is the main thing that's causing you to go back to smoking. Can you tell me, what do you usually do to try and calm down when you're feeling stressed?
I think I need something for the stress specifically, not just the craving.

You feel like you need something to specifically address the stress, not just manage the craving. Would you be open to trying a short walk or some light physical activity when you're feeling stressed?
I smoke. Have been for about 10 years.
You've been smoking for 10 years, that's a long time. Can you tell me when you usually smoke, is it when you're stressed, bored, or in certain situations?
I do not know. Sometimes after food I guess.

You're feeling a bit uncertain about your smoking habits, and it's okay, that's the first step to understanding them. What comes to mind when you think about quitting smoking - is there something specific that's making you want to stop now?
My wife wants me to stop.

Your wife wanting you to stop is a strong motivator, that's good to know. Now, since you mentioned that eating is a time when you usually smoke, would it be helpful to find something to do right after meals to break that habit?
I have not really tried anything.
It sounds like you're open to trying something new to break the habit of smoking after meals. Would you be willing to try taking a short walk after eating to distract yourself from the urge to smoke?
I am not sure I am ready.
You're still thinking about trying something new to break the habit of smoking after meals. Can you imagine what would make you feel more ready to give it a try?

I have been smoking bidis for 25 years, about 15 a day. I want to quit because of my health and my grandchildren. I tried nicotine gum before but did not like the taste. I usually smoke after meals and when I am stressed.

You've been smoking bidis for 25 years, and now you're motivated to quit for the health of yourself and your grandchildren - that takes a lot of courage. Can you think of any small steps you could take to change your routine after meals, maybe something as simple as taking a short walk or doing a different activity to help distract you from craving a bidi?
Yes I am serious about quitting this time.
I'm so proud of the progress you've made already, and I have no doubt you'll continue to move forward in your journey to quit smoking.
The stress is the hardest part. Work and money problems.

You're right, stress is a significant challenge for you, and it's great that you're willing to acknowledge it. Have you ever tried taking a short walk outside when you're feeling stressed, even if it's just for a minute or two, to see if that helps you calm down?
I have not really tried anything for stress specifically.

It sounds like managing stress is a crucial part for your quit plan, and taking a short walk outside can be a simple yet effective way to calm down and break the cycle of reaching for a bidi. Would you be open to giving that a try and seeing how it affects your stress levels?
Maybe I can try something for after meals first. That seems easier.

You're feeling more confident about tackling the after-meal trigger first - that's a great step. Let's try breaking that automatic link between meals and bidis; would you be willing to get up from the table right after finishing a meal and do something else, like brushing your teeth or taking a short walk, before even thinking about lighting up?
I smoke cigarettes, about 10 a day for 8 years.
You've been smoking for 8 years now. What are some situations or emotions that tend to make you reach for a cigarette?
Stress and boredom mostly. Also after eating.

You mentioned that stress and boredom often lead you to smoke, and also after eating. Does moving to a different room or taking a short walk after meals help you feel more in control and reduce the urge to smoke?
I want to quit for my health mainly.

You're motivated to quit for your health, which is a great reason. Considering your triggers, would having a trusted friend or family member with you when you're feeling stressed, bored, or after eating help you stay on track?
What do you think I should do? Just tell me what works.
When you're feeling stressed or bored, and you want to reach for a cigarette, what if you tried taking a short walk outside to get some fresh air instead?
Okay I will try that. What if it does not work though?
You've taken a courageous step forward, and I'm confident that you'll find a path that works for you.
I use chewing tobacco, about 5 pouches a day for 12 years.
It sounds like a long history with chewing tobacco, and I'm here to support you in making a change. Can you tell me, what do you think triggers you to use chewing tobacco throughout the day?
After meals and during work mainly. It helps me concentrate.

I see that you've been using chewing tobacco to help you focus during work and also after meals. You mentioned it helps you concentrate, which I think is a crucial part of your day. Can you tell me, what would be the most important reason for you to quit using chewing tobacco?
I want to stop because my dentist said there is some damage to my gums.

It sounds like your dentist's feedback about gum damage has really motivated you to make a change. Would you be open to trying nicotine gum as a potential replacement for chewing tobacco during times when you normally use it, like after meals or during work?
I tried stopping once but got very irritable and could not focus at work.

It sounds like stopping cold turkey didn't work for you because it affected your mood and ability to focus. What was it about that experience that you think might be helpful to build on this time around?
Maybe I can reduce first and see how it goes.

You're considering reducing your use of chewing tobacco first, and that's a good place to start. What specific steps do you think you'll take to create a plan for gradually reducing your use, like you mentioned earlier?
I smoke about 20 cigarettes a day. Been smoking for 15 years.
It sounds like you've developed a pretty heavy smoking habit over the years. What time of day do you usually start smoking?
I smoke when I feel like it. There is no specific trigger.

It sounds like you have a fair amount of flexibility with when you smoke, which can be a challenge when trying to address the habit. Can you tell me more about what you mean by "when I feel like it" - is it when you're bored, anxious, or just need a break?
I am here because my doctor sent me. I do not particularly want to quit.
It sounds like you're feeling a bit pressured into being here, which can make the process harder. What do you think would make you feel motivated to quit, if anything?
I have tried before and it does not work. Quitting just makes me miserable.

It sounds like your past attempts to quit have been tough on you, and you're feeling a bit hopeless about the process. Can you tell me more about what exactly made you feel miserable during those attempts?
Fine. What is the point of all these questions anyway.
It sounds like you're feeling a bit frustrated with the process. Can you tell me what you're hoping to get out of coming here, even if it's not necessarily quitting smoking?
I have been smoking for 7 years, about 12 cigarettes a day.
That's a significant amount of time and cigarettes per day. What's a typical day like for you - do you smoke at the same times every day, like when you wake up or during your breaks at work?
I smoke when I am anxious and in social situations with other smokers.

It sounds like social situations with other smokers are a really tough trigger for you. Can you think of a way to gradually reduce your exposure to those situations, like taking a different route to work or avoiding certain social gatherings for now?
I want to quit because I am planning to have a baby soon.
I'm glad to hear you're motivated to quit for your baby's sake. Have you tried quitting before, and if so, what did you learn from those experiences that might help us now?
I tried cold turkey twice. The second time I lasted 3 months then relapsed at a party.

You've tried quitting cold turkey before and it didn't quite work for you. Are you willing to consider another approach this time, like taking it one day at a time rather than trying to quit all at once?
Social situations are the hardest. I do not know how to say no.

It sounds like saying no to cigarettes in social situations feels really challenging for you. Can you think of someone in your life, like a close friend or family member, who might be willing to support you in staying smoke-free at social gatherings?
Okay I think I can try having a response ready when someone offers me a cigarette.

You're feeling more confident about having a response ready when someone offers you a cigarette. Can you imagine how it would feel to give that response in a real situation, and what kind of support system you might need in place to help you stick to it?
Yes that sounds like a good plan. I will try it this weekend.
You've taken a big step forward today, and I wish you all the best in putting your newfound confidence into practice.
